does: This note develops conceptual foundations for mechanistic interpretability by arguing that identifying and analyzing variables—meaningful latent features that neural networks compute—is central to understanding model internals. The author emphasizes that interpretability depends critically on finding the right representational basis: the same underlying computation can appear incomprehensible in one basis but transparent in another. The work sketches how mechanistic interpretability progresses by discovering which variables a model uses, how they're computed through layers, and how they combine to produce outputs, proposing that this variable-centric approach provides a structured framework for decomposing neural network behavior into human-understandable components.
connects: This work builds on the growing mechanistic interpretability research program that seeks to reverse-engineer neural networks by tracing information flow and identifying interpretable features. It extends prior work on feature visualization, activation analysis, and circuit discovery by foregrounding the theoretical question of what makes a basis interpretable rather than merely observable. The note engages with debates about whether neural networks learn discrete, modular computations versus distributed representations, and connects to broader efforts in neuroscience and AI that pursue similar variable-discovery strategies for understanding complex systems.
enables: By articulating the importance of finding interpretable bases, this work provides a conceptual roadmap for developing better automated and semi-automated methods to discover meaningful variables in neural networks. It suggests future research should focus on: developing principled ways to identify which bases are interpretable, creating tools to systematically search over candidate variable systems, and determining when different bases are equivalently valid. These insights could enable more scalable approaches to auditing model behavior, detecting deceptive or misaligned computation, and building confidence in AI systems by rendering their internal operations legible to humans.
